stem: The function \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z^{2}+([[b]])z+([[c]])}{(z-([[p]]))^{2}} \) has an isolated singularity at \( z=[[p]] \), a pole of order \( 2 \) (a double pole). Compute the residue \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} f \).
distractor_rationales: This is the numerator evaluated at \( z=[[p]] \) (the value of \( f \)'s numerator), not the derivative the double-pole residue formula requires.
This doubles the numerator's value at \( z=[[p]] \) instead of differentiating it.
This uses only twice the leading coefficient, ignoring the point \( z=[[p]] \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This has the wrong sign from the correct residue.

stem: The function \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z^{3}+([[b]])z^{2}+([[c]])z+([[d]])}{(z-([[p]]))^{3}} \) has an isolated singularity at \( z=[[p]] \), a pole of order \( 3 \). Compute the residue \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]} f \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the FIRST derivative (the double-pole formula) instead of the second derivative that an order-\( 3 \) pole requires.
This forgets to divide by \( 2! = 2 \).
This is the second derivative alone, forgetting the required \( 1/2! \) factor from the residue formula.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This is off by one from the correct residue.
This has the wrong sign from the correct residue.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z^{2}+([[b]])z+([[c]])}{z-([[p]])} \). Since the numerator also vanishes at \( z=[[p]] \), the singularity at \( z=[[p]] \) is removable rather than a pole. Compute \( \displaystyle\lim_{z\to [[p]]} f(z) \), the value that removes the singularity.
solution: Since \( z=[[p]] \) is a root of the numerator, factor it out: \( ([[a]])z^{2}+([[b]])z+([[c]]) = (z-([[p]]))\left(([[a]])z + \left(([[a]])([[p]])+([[b]])\right)\right) \), so \( f(z) = ([[a]])z+\left(([[a]])([[p]])+([[b]])\right) \) for \( z\ne[[p]] \), and the limit as \( z\to[[p]] \) is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the numerator's value at \( z=[[p]] \), which is \( 0/0 \) and not the removable limit.
This uses only the leading coefficient times \( p \), omitting the linear coefficient \( b \).
This doubles the leading term's contribution instead of following the factorization.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This has the wrong sign from the correct limit.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(([[k]])(z-([[p]]))\right)}{z-([[p]])} \). This has a removable singularity at \( z=[[p]] \) (the numerator also vanishes there). Compute \( \displaystyle\lim_{z\to [[p]]} f(z) \), the value that removes the singularity.
solution: Using \( \displaystyle\lim_{w\to 0}\dfrac{\sin(kw)}{w} = k \) with \( w=z-([[p]]) \), the limit is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the classic \( \lim \sin(w)/w = 1 \) fact applied without accounting for the scale factor \( k \) inside the sine.
This mistakes the removable value for the singular point's numerator value, which is \( 0 \) but not the limit of the quotient.
This multiplies \( k \) by the singular point \( p \), which plays no role in this limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This has the wrong sign from the correct limit.
